concepts.name: Divisibility
concepts.intuition_gloss: One number fits into another a whole number of times, with nothing left over.
concepts.formal_gloss: For integers a and b, a divides b (written a | b) iff there exists an integer k with b = ak; divisibility is a partial order on the positive integers.

concepts.name: Division Algorithm
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any division of whole numbers leaves a unique quotient and a remainder strictly smaller than the divisor.
concepts.formal_gloss: For integers a and b with b > 0, there exist unique integers q and r with a = bq + r and 0 <= r < b.
sources: grounds both the Euclidean algorithm and congruence — high leverage

concepts.name: Greatest Common Divisor & LCM
concepts.intuition_gloss: The largest number that fits evenly into two given numbers — and its mirror, the smallest number both fit into.
concepts.formal_gloss: gcd(a,b) is the largest positive integer dividing both a and b; lcm(a,b) is the smallest positive common multiple, with gcd(a,b) * lcm(a,b) = |ab|.

concepts.name: Euclidean Algorithm
concepts.intuition_gloss: Repeatedly divide and keep the remainder, and the last nonzero remainder is the gcd — one of the oldest algorithms still in daily use.
concepts.formal_gloss: The iteration gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b) terminating at gcd(a,0) = a, computing the gcd in O(log min(a,b)) division steps.

concepts.name: Bézout's Identity
concepts.intuition_gloss: The gcd of two numbers can always be built by adding and subtracting whole-number multiples of them.
concepts.formal_gloss: For integers a and b, there exist integers x and y with ax + by = gcd(a,b); ax + by = c is solvable iff gcd(a,b) divides c.
sources: key lemma for FTA and modular inverses — high leverage in mathlib dependency structure

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
concepts.intuition_gloss: Every whole number is built from primes in exactly one way, like a chemical compound from its elements.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every integer greater than 1 is a product of primes, and this factorization is unique up to the order of the factors.

resources.title: Hardy & Wright, An Introduction to the Theory of Numbers, ch. 1-2

sources: its failure in general rings motivates algebraic number theory

concepts.name: Sieve of Eratosthenes
concepts.intuition_gloss: Cross out every multiple of each prime in turn, and the numbers left standing are the primes.
concepts.formal_gloss: An algorithm listing all primes up to N by iteratively marking multiples of each prime p <= sqrt(N), in O(N log log N) operations; the prototype for analytic sieve methods.
sources: gateway to sieve theory (Princeton Companion IV.2 Analytic Number Theory)

concepts.name: Fermat's Little Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Raise any number to a prime power on a clock with a prime number of hours, and it cycles back to where it started.
concepts.formal_gloss: If p is prime and p does not divide a, then a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p); equivalently a^p ≡ a (mod p) for all integers a.

resources.title: Silverman, A Friendly Introduction to Number Theory (free chapters)

sources: special case of Lagrange's theorem — bridge to group theory

concepts.name: Euler's Totient & Euler's Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Count how many numbers below n share no factor with n — that count controls how powers cycle on the mod-n clock.
concepts.formal_gloss: φ(n) = |{1 <= k <= n : gcd(k,n) = 1}| = |(Z/nZ)*|; Euler's theorem states a^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n) whenever gcd(a,n) = 1.
sources: load-bearing for RSA and for arithmetic-function theory

concepts.name: Chinese Remainder Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: If clocks of coprime sizes each show a chosen time, exactly one number on the combined mega-clock matches them all.
concepts.formal_gloss: For pairwise coprime moduli n1..nk, the ring Z/(n1...nk)Z is isomorphic to the product of the rings Z/niZ, so any system x ≡ ai (mod ni) has a unique solution mod the product.
sources: undergrad NT and abstract algebra syllabi (ring-product form)

concepts.name: Multiplicative Order & Primitive Roots
concepts.intuition_gloss: Keep multiplying a number by itself on the mod-n clock and watch how long before it first returns to 1 — some special numbers take the longest possible tour.
concepts.formal_gloss: The order of a modulo n is the least k >= 1 with a^k ≡ 1 (mod n); a primitive root is an element of order φ(n), i.e., a generator of the cyclic group (Z/nZ)*.
sources: underlies Diffie-Hellman and the discrete-log problem

concepts.name: Quadratic Residues & Reciprocity
concepts.intuition_gloss: Whether p is a perfect square on q's clock and whether q is one on p's clock turn out to be secretly linked — Gauss's 'golden theorem'.
concepts.formal_gloss: For odd primes p and q, the Legendre symbols satisfy (p/q)(q/p) = (-1)^((p-1)/2 * (q-1)/2), governing solvability of x^2 ≡ a (mod p).

resources.title: Ireland & Rosen, A Classical Introduction to Modern Number Theory, ch. 5

sources: Princeton Companion — first step toward class field theory and Langlands

concepts.name: Arithmetic Functions & Möbius Inversion
concepts.intuition_gloss: Functions defined on the whole numbers — like 'count the divisors' or 'sum them' — that inherit the primes' multiplication structure and can be un-summed by a clever inversion.
concepts.formal_gloss: Functions f: N → C, with the multiplicative ones determined by prime-power values; under Dirichlet convolution they form a ring in which Möbius inversion recovers f from g(n) = Σ_{d|n} f(d).

resources.title: Apostol, Introduction to Analytic Number Theory, ch. 2

sources: the algebraic front end of analytic number theory

concepts.name: Diophantine Equations
concepts.intuition_gloss: Equations where only whole-number answers count — a tiny change of rules that turns easy algebra into some of the hardest problems ever posed.
concepts.formal_gloss: Polynomial equations over Z whose integer (or rational) solutions are sought; Hilbert's tenth problem — a general decision algorithm — is unsolvable (Matiyasevich, 1970).

concepts.name: Pythagorean Triples
concepts.intuition_gloss: Whole-number right triangles like 3-4-5 — and a complete recipe that generates every one of them.
concepts.formal_gloss: Integer solutions of a^2 + b^2 = c^2; every primitive triple is (m^2 - n^2, 2mn, m^2 + n^2) for coprime m > n of opposite parity.

resources.title: Silverman, A Friendly Introduction to Number Theory, ch. 2-3 (free)

sources: the prototype of a completely solved Diophantine problem

concepts.name: Pell's Equation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Hunting whole-number points on the hyperbola x² − D·y² = 1 — the solutions form an endless ladder generated by a single fundamental step.
concepts.formal_gloss: The equation x^2 - Dy^2 = 1 for nonsquare D, whose solutions form an infinite cyclic group generated by the fundamental solution, found via the continued fraction of sqrt(D).
sources: units of real quadratic fields — bridge to Dirichlet's unit theorem

concepts.name: Continued Fractions
concepts.intuition_gloss: Write a number as a nested staircase of fractions, and each landing is the best possible fraction approximation so far.
concepts.formal_gloss: Expressions a0 + 1/(a1 + 1/(a2 + ...)); the convergents p_n/q_n give best rational approximations, and the expansion is eventually periodic iff the number is a quadratic irrational.

concepts.name: Irrationality & Transcendence
concepts.intuition_gloss: Some numbers, like √2, can never be written as fractions — and some, like π and e, can't even be captured by any polynomial equation.
concepts.formal_gloss: A real number is irrational if it lies outside Q, and transcendental if it is a root of no nonzero polynomial with rational coefficients; algebraic numbers are countable, so almost all reals are transcendental.

resources.title: Square root of 2 is irrational — Wikipedia

sources: the √2 proof is the canonical first contradiction proof

concepts.name: Fermat's Last Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: No cube splits into two cubes, and likewise for every higher power — a margin note that took 358 years and a whole new bridge of mathematics to prove.
concepts.formal_gloss: For n >= 3, x^n + y^n = z^n has no positive integer solutions; proved by Wiles (1995) via the modularity of semistable elliptic curves.

resources.title: BBC Horizon: Fermat's Last Theorem (Simon Singh documentary)

sources: Princeton Companion V — theorem as landmark
statement is elementary; placement reflects its role connecting territories

concepts.name: Distribution of Primes
concepts.intuition_gloss: Primes look random up close but obey a smooth global law — near N, roughly one number in every ln(N) is prime.
concepts.formal_gloss: π(x) ~ x/ln(x) (the prime number theorem, Hadamard & de la Vallée Poussin 1896); Dirichlet's theorem gives infinitely many primes in every arithmetic progression a mod n with gcd(a,n) = 1.
sources: Princeton Companion IV.2 Analytic Number Theory
Euclid's infinitude proof is the level-2 on-ramp; PNT sets the level

concepts.name: Riemann Zeta Function & the Riemann Hypothesis
concepts.intuition_gloss: A single smooth function whose hidden zeros conduct the rhythm of the primes — and the million-dollar conjecture that they all line up on one vertical line.
concepts.formal_gloss: ζ(s) = Σ n^(-s) = Π_p (1 - p^(-s))^(-1) for Re(s) > 1, continued analytically to C \ {1}; the Riemann hypothesis asserts all nontrivial zeros have real part 1/2.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown: Visualizing the Riemann zeta function and analytic continuation
LMFDB — L-functions and Modular Forms Database

sources: Princeton Companion IV.2 & V (RH as open problem)

concepts.name: Primality Testing
concepts.intuition_gloss: Deciding whether a huge number is prime without trying to factor it — fast randomized tests make 600-digit verdicts routine.
concepts.formal_gloss: Algorithms deciding primality: Fermat/Miller-Rabin probabilistic tests based on a^(n-1) mod n, and the deterministic polynomial-time AKS algorithm (2002) placing PRIMES in P.
sources: Crandall & Pomerance, Prime Numbers: A Computational Perspective

concepts.name: Integer Factorization Problem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Multiplying two big primes is instant; recovering them from the product seems to need astronomical time — an asymmetry the digital world leans on.
concepts.formal_gloss: The problem of computing the prime factorization of n; best known classical algorithms (general number field sieve) are subexponential, while Shor's quantum algorithm factors in polynomial time.

concepts.name: RSA & Number-Theoretic Cryptography
concepts.intuition_gloss: Euler's 250-year-old clock theorem, run in reverse with a secret key, lets strangers exchange locked messages over open channels.
concepts.formal_gloss: RSA encrypts by c = m^e mod n for n = pq and decrypts by m = c^d mod n where ed ≡ 1 (mod φ(n)); security rests on the hardness of factoring n, and Diffie-Hellman analogously on discrete logarithms.
sources: the standard 'why number theory matters' payoff in modern curricula

concepts.name: Gaussian Integers
concepts.intuition_gloss: Whole numbers spread out on a grid in the complex plane, where 5 stops being prime because it splits as (2+i)(2−i).
concepts.formal_gloss: The ring Z[i] = {a + bi : a,b in Z}, a Euclidean domain with unique factorization; an odd prime p splits in Z[i] iff p ≡ 1 (mod 4), yielding Fermat's two-squares theorem.

resources.title: Ireland & Rosen, ch. 1 (unique factorization in Z[i])

sources: the canonical first example of extending arithmetic beyond Z

concepts.name: Algebraic Number Fields & Ideal Factorization
concepts.intuition_gloss: Enlarge the whole numbers with roots of polynomials; unique factorization can break, but it is rescued by factoring 'ideal' numbers instead.
concepts.formal_gloss: A number field is a finite extension K/Q with ring of integers O_K, a Dedekind domain in which every nonzero ideal factors uniquely into prime ideals; the class group measures the failure of unique element factorization.

resources.title: Milne, Algebraic Number Theory (free course notes)

sources: Princeton Companion IV.1 Algebraic Numbers (Granville/Mazur treatment)

concepts.name: p-adic Numbers
concepts.intuition_gloss: A parallel universe of numbers where two integers are 'close' when their difference is divisible by a high power of p — completing the rationals in a totally different direction from the reals.
concepts.formal_gloss: Q_p is the completion of Q under the p-adic absolute value |x|_p = p^(-v_p(x)); Ostrowski's theorem says R and the Q_p are the only completions of Q, and Hensel's lemma lifts solutions mod p to Z_p.

concepts.name: Elliptic Curves
concepts.intuition_gloss: A cubic curve where a chord-and-tangent game turns the points into an addition system — the meeting point of geometry, arithmetic, and cryptography.
concepts.formal_gloss: A smooth projective curve y^2 = x^3 + ax + b (char ≠ 2,3) with a marked point, whose points over any field form an abelian group; over Q the group is finitely generated (Mordell-Weil).

resources.title: Silverman, The Arithmetic of Elliptic Curves

sources: Princeton Companion III.21 & IV Arithmetic Geometry
BSD is a Clay Millennium Problem

concepts.name: Modular Forms
concepts.intuition_gloss: Functions on the upper half-plane with an outrageous amount of symmetry, whose coefficient sequences mysteriously encode deep arithmetic — the 'fifth operation of arithmetic'.
concepts.formal_gloss: Holomorphic functions f on the upper half-plane satisfying f((az+b)/(cz+d)) = (cz+d)^k f(z) for matrices in a congruence subgroup of SL2(Z), plus growth conditions; the modularity theorem attaches one to every rational elliptic curve.

resources.title: Diamond & Shurman, A First Course in Modular Forms
